windows.transcript: because you could cause frosting and adhesion problems. So I've just reheated my wax and I'm going to pour it just a little bit hotter
windows.old_description: Trimming the wooden wick on a finished candle.
windows.embed_text_v2: pouring warm tan candle wax from white jug into three white ceramic cups fitted with metal wick-centering bars, amber wax filling cups on heart-patterned tray, wiping drip
windows.summary_v2: Overhead, warm tan candle wax is poured from a jug into three white ceramic cups fitted with metal wick-centering bars on a patterned tray.

seconds_v2.desc: Top-down view of three white ceramic cups on a patterned tray, each spanned by a metal wick-centering bar; pale tan wax is poured from a white jug into the leftmost cup, which holds amber liquid wax. A red 'STEP 9' overlay circles the right cup.

Pouring continues into the left cup, the stream of warm tan wax splashing against the already-filled amber pool; the middle and right cups hold set lighter wax. 'STEP 9' overlay still visible on the right.
The jug tilts back as the left cup nears full with glossy amber wax; bubbles dot its surface. Middle and right cups remain filled with paler wax, wick bars centered across each.
Brief pause in pouring; the left cup brims with reflective amber wax while the jug hovers at the rim. Three cups sit aligned on the heart-patterned tray.
A white cloth or paper is dabbed near the left cup's rim to wipe a drip; the left cup is full of amber wax, the other two hold lighter-colored wax under their wick bars.
Still overhead shot of the three filled cups; the left holds darker amber wax, the middle and right paler tan wax, each centered by a metal wick holder on the patterned tray.
Camera shifts slightly; the three white cups of wax sit in a row, the left one's amber surface still wet and glossy, wick bars resting across all three rims.
The white pouring jug re-enters at top, tipping toward the middle cup to top it off; left cup remains full of amber wax, right cup of pale tan wax.
Wax pours from the jug into the middle cup, the tan stream filling it under its wick bar; the left and right cups already hold their poured wax on the tray.
